exercises.title: Editor + the shell command
exercises.prompt: Install VS Code, enable the `code` command so you can open folders from the terminal, and install the core extensions this lesson names for AI work. Verify `code` works from the shell.
exercises.hint: In VS Code: Command Palette → 'Shell Command: Install code command in PATH'. Core extensions: Python, Pylance, Jupyter, GitLens.
exercises.solution:
```bash
code --version          # confirms the CLI is on PATH
code .                  # opens the current folder
# install extensions from the terminal:
code --install-extension ms-python.python
code --install-extension ms-python.vscode-pylance
code --install-extension ms-toolsai.jupyter
code --install-extension eamodio.gitlens
```

The `code .` habit — open a project folder straight from the terminal — is small but you'll do it thousands of times.

exercises.title: Format-on-save + type checks
exercises.prompt: Configure your editor so Python files auto-format on every save and basic type errors show inline as you type. Put the config in the project's `.vscode/settings.json` so it travels with the repo.

exercises.title: Edit on a GPU box over SSH
exercises.prompt: Set up Remote SSH so you can open a folder on a remote GPU machine and edit/run code there as if it were local, with the heavy compute staying on the remote. Outline the steps and why this beats editing locally + scp-ing files.
exercises.hint: The Remote-SSH extension; an entry in `~/.ssh/config`; then 'Connect to Host'. The VS Code server runs remotely; your UI is local.
exercises.solution:
```sshconfig
# ~/.ssh/config
Host gpu-box
    HostName 203.0.113.10
    User ubuntu
    IdentityFile ~/.ssh/id_ed25519
```
Then in VS Code: install **Remote - SSH**, Command Palette → *Remote-SSH: Connect to Host* → `gpu-box`, and open the remote folder.

VS Code installs a lightweight server on the remote; your editor UI runs locally but the **filesystem, terminal, Python interpreter, and GPU all live on the remote**. Beats local-edit + `scp`: no constant file syncing, the integrated terminal already has the GPU and CUDA, and `torch.cuda.is_available()` is True without copying anything. Your laptop stays a thin client.
